title: The Molecular Basis of Genetic Variation

lesson.learn: Genetic variation refers to the differences in nucleotide sequences between individuals, populations, and species, resulting from mutations, gene flow, and genetic drift.
lesson.simpler: Genetic variation occurs when small changes, known as mutations, occur in the DNA sequence. This can result in differences in gene expression and protein function.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Genetic variation is a fundamental aspect of genetic biology, essential for adaptation and evolution.
lesson.deeperDive: Recent studies have demonstrated the critical role of genetic variation in disease susceptibility, agricultural productivity, and biotechnology. For example, the BRCA1 gene variant has been linked to breast and ovarian cancer risk. Similarly, crop yield and resistance to pests are influenced by genetic variation in plant genomes. Gene editing technologies, such as CRISPR-Cas9, are being used to manipulate genetic variation for therapeutic and agricultural purposes.
lesson.funFact: The first recorded example of genetic variation was observed by Gregor Mendel in 1865, in his experiments with pea plants. Mendel's work demonstrated the existence of discrete genetic traits and provided the foundation for modern genetics.

people: + James Watson - Co-discoverer of DNA structure
+ Francis Collins - Director of the National Institutes of Health (NIH)
places: + University of Cambridge - Discovery site of DNA's double helix structure
+ Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory - Location of the Human Genome Project
+ National Institutes of Health (NIH) - Primary funding source for genetic research
+ The Sanger Centre - Genomics research facility
+ Harvard University - Hub for genetic research and innovation
years: + 1865 - Publication of Mendel's paper on pea plant variation
+ 1953 - Description of DNA structure by Watson and Crick
+ 2003 - Completion of the Human Genome Project
+ 2012 - First human genome sequenced using CRISPR-Cas9
concepts: + Mutation: Random change in nucleotide sequence
+ Gene flow: Transfer of genes between populations
+ Genetic drift: Random changes in allele frequencies
+ Heritability: Measure of genetic influence on traits
+ Epigenetics: Study of gene expression and modification
+ Genomics: Field of study focused on the structure and function of genomes.
